calendar.matters:
MASTER CALENDAR COURT
Off Calendar Jun-23-2021 Vacated

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Dec-07-2020 Set For Trial On Jul-12-21 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 206

Defendant Geoffrey Palermos Notice Of Motion And Motion For Protective Order To Continue Deposition
Off Calendar Oct-13-2020 Off Calendar

Order To Show Cause Re: Preliminary Injunction
Off Calendar Aug-12-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Attys. SARA T. SCHNEIDER, Esq. and VICTOR DANHI, Esq. of Arent Fox LLP (Tel: 415-757-5500) for plaintiff German Motors Corporation;
Atty. WILLIAM W. BUNTING III, Esq. of Bunting, Drayton & Alward LLP Bunting, Drayton & Alward LLP (Tel: 415-617-0244) for defendant GMP Cars, LLC.

Order To Show Cause Re: Preliminary Injunction.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling: Plaintiff German Motors Corporation's order to show cause regarding preliminary injunction is granted.

Defendant GMP and its agents are immediately enjoined from selling, transferring, mortgaging, or otherwise encumbering any of the collateral that is presently at the auto repair shop except in the ordinary course of business and with plaintiff's written consent. This collateral is the non-highlighted equipment listed in exhibit 1 of the Schneider reply declaration. Plaintiff shall prepare an order that specifically lists this equipment.

This court considers two interrelated questions in deciding whether to issue a preliminary injunction: "1) are the plaintiffs likely to suffer greater injury from a denial of the injunction than the defendants are likely to suffer from its grant; and 2) is there a reasonable probability that the plaintiffs will prevail on the merits...." (Robbins v. Superior Court (1985) 38 Cal.3d 199, 206.)

Plaintiff demonstrates a likelihood of prevailing on the merits. Plaintiff shows that GMP has breached the parties' promissory note and owes currently over $3,000,000 to plaintiff. (Mayora Dec., pars. 6-8.) GMP does not contend otherwise. Under Sec 2 of the parties' Security Agreement, plaintiff has a security interest in GMP's fixtures, personal property, etc. ("collateral"). Plaintiff is entitled to an injunction to protect its security. GMP does not convincingly demonstrate that plaintiff breached the parties' Parts Purchase and Referral Agreement. At any rate, such a breach of a separate agreement might allow a setoff, but it does not excuse GMP's performance under the promissory note.

Plaintiff demonstrates irreparable harm and that the balance of equities and harms weighs in its favor. Persuasive evidence establishes that equipment at GMP's shop, which constitutes part of the "collateral" under the parties' Security Agreement, has disappeared and the shop is running on fumes. (Schmitt Reply Dec., pars. 5 & 7.) GMP admits that the shop has experienced a severe revenue downturn. (Lim Dec., pars. 6-7.) A suit for money damages alone is an inadequate remedy. "An injunction against disposing of property is proper if disposal would render the final judgment ineffectual." (Heckmann v. Ahmanson (1985) 168 Cal.App.3d 119, 138 [affirming order granting preliminary injunction impose trust on profit from corporate transaction to prevent the dissipation or disappearance of the profit during the pendency of the action].)

Under the instant circumstances, an injunction is necessary so plaintiff is not left with "a naked claim for damages." (Id.) The existence of allegedly superior third-party security interests in the collateral is not a basis to deny plaintiff relief. At worst, the proposed injunction protects those priority holders too.

Pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure Sec 529, plaintiff shall post a $5,000 bond.

Order signed. Preliminary injunction signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Rose Miller, CSR#5041, email: RMillerCSR@gmail.com (302/EPS)
